source_materials: https://www.aihr.com/blog/hr-data-analytics/ (AIHR: 5 Python Libraries You Need in HR)
meta.difficulty_scale: 1 (easiest) to 7 (hardest); the ladder picks one question per tier so difficulty ramps with the round
meta.note: Seed bank only. Expand freely; the engine rotates by seeding on contestant id so no two contestants get the same round.

questions.question: In the deck, which library is nicknamed 'The Organizer'?
questions.explanation: Pandas is 'The Organizer'. It loads, cleans, and analyzes tabular HR exports from an HRIS, ATS, or payroll system. Its main structure is the DataFrame, a smarter spreadsheet you command with code.

questions.question: The deck's mnemonic is 'clean, calculate, show, check, predict'. Which library does 'show' refer to?
questions.explanation: Seaborn is 'The Illustrator' and maps to 'show'. It turns workforce data into charts leaders can read, like box plots and heatmaps. Clean = Pandas, calculate = NumPy, check = Statsmodels, predict = Scikit-learn.

questions.question: What is the name of Pandas' main data structure, the thing that holds your HR table in memory?
questions.explanation: A DataFrame is Pandas' core structure: rows and columns like a spreadsheet, but you filter, group, merge, and calculate against it with short readable code.

questions.question: Which library does the deck call 'The Calculator', the math engine that Pandas is built on top of?
questions.explanation: NumPy (Numerical Python) is 'The Calculator'. When you run a Pandas calculation, NumPy often does the math underneath. It handles fast, reliable math on arrays of numbers.

questions.question: Which one line computes the overall attrition rate as a percentage in Pandas?
questions.explanation: df['Attrition'].value_counts(normalize=True) * 100 counts Yes/No shares and scales to a percentage. That is your overall attrition rate, the starting point for any turnover analysis.

questions.question: According to the deck, why is Excel a weak starting point for HR analytics?
options: It cannot open more than 100 rows
It slows on large datasets, lacks practical machine learning, and charts need manual cleanup
questions.explanation: Excel is a fine start but taps out: it slows on large workforce exports, machine learning is out of practical reach, and charts need manual cleanup before sharing. Python removes those limits.

questions.question: Which Pandas method groups rows by a column, for example to compare attrition by department?
questions.explanation: groupby() groups rows by a column. df.groupby('Department')['Attrition'].mean() gives the attrition rate per department. fillna fills blanks, describe summarizes, merge joins tables.

questions.question: To get the 25th, 50th, and 75th percentile of employee salaries, which NumPy call do you use?
questions.explanation: np.percentile(df['MonthlyIncome'], [25, 50, 75]) returns instant salary benchmarks. The deck shows this as NumPy's 'Calculator' role: fast, reliable workforce math.

questions.question: NumPy is best described as what kind of tool?
options: A charting library built on Matplotlib
A library for fast math on arrays of numbers
questions.explanation: NumPy is a library for fast math on arrays of numbers. Like the engine in a car: not glamorous, but nothing moves without it. It keeps Pandas' calculations fast and accurate.

questions.question: Statsmodels is nicknamed 'The Auditor' because it provides what that makes HR findings defensible?
options: p-values, confidence intervals, and regression outputs
A faster way to clean data
questions.explanation: Statsmodels gives p-values, confidence intervals, and regression output. That turns 'this looks like a gap' into 'this is a gap, and here is the proof', the evidence that stands up to finance, legal, or a regulator.

questions.question: Seaborn is built on top of which lower level plotting library?
questions.explanation: Seaborn is built on Matplotlib. Matplotlib gives full control but needs lots of code; Seaborn focuses on statistical charts and produces clean output with far less effort.

questions.question: Scikit-learn moves HR analytics from 'turnover was 18% last year' to what kind of statement?
options: These employees may be more likely to leave next quarter
questions.explanation: Scikit-learn is 'The Forecaster'. It moves you from describing the past to predicting the future: from 'turnover was 18%' to 'these employees are at higher flight risk next quarter'.

questions.question: Which Scikit-learn function splits your data into training and test sets so you can check model honesty?
questions.explanation: train_test_split holds out a portion of data the model never sees in training, then tests on it. That is how you check whether the model genuinely learned or just memorized. random_state=42 makes the split reproducible.

questions.question: In Scikit-learn, what do LabelEncoder and OneHotEncoder do to columns like Department or Gender?
options: Turn text categories into numbers a model can use
questions.explanation: Models need numbers, not text. LabelEncoder and OneHotEncoder convert categories like Department or Gender into numeric form the algorithm can train on.

questions.question: Which Seaborn call draws a correlation heatmap across numeric HR variables?
questions.explanation: sns.heatmap(df.corr(numeric_only=True), annot=True) shows which numeric factors move together with attrition. The deck calls this Seaborn's 'Illustrator' job: patterns leaders can read at a glance.

questions.question: The deck says a p-value below what number means a pattern is unlikely to be chance alone?
questions.explanation: A p-value below 0.05 is the magic number: the observed pattern is unlikely to be random. That is the bar 'The Auditor' (Statsmodels) uses to call a finding real.

questions.question: The deck argues 'Findings that nobody understands change nothing.' This makes the case for which library?
questions.explanation: That line is the case for Seaborn, 'The Illustrator'. Analysis only creates value when a CFO or CHRO can read it at a glance, and Seaborn is how your analysis earns its meeting slot.

questions.question: In pay-equity work, the code smf.ols('MonthlyIncome ~ Gender + YearsAtCompany + Education') is an example of what?
options: Ordinary least squares regression that controls for fair factors
questions.explanation: It is ordinary least squares regression. By including YearsAtCompany and Education alongside Gender, it asks: does a salary gap remain after the fair factors are counted? That is Statsmodels' 'Auditor' role.

questions.question: After training a Random Forest attrition model, which output shows which levers actually drive attrition in your data?
questions.explanation: feature_importances_ ranks which variables matter most. The deck calls this 'boardroom gold': it shows which factors actually drive attrition in your data, not just that a pattern exists.

questions.question: Grouping by 'OverTime' and then running value_counts on 'Attrition' reveals what workforce signal?
options: The effect of overtime on turnover
questions.explanation: df.groupby('OverTime')['Attrition'].value_counts(normalize=True) shows how much more often overtime employees leave. It is a textbook Pandas 'Organizer' move for surfacing a turnover driver.
